step_by_step_thinking:
The zygomatic arch is composed of both bone and sutures. Fractures can occur along these bony or fibrous structures, but due to its prominence and structural complexity, fractures commonly involve actual bone rather than just sutural lines. The posterior portion tends to be more robustly supported by surrounding muscles and ligaments compared to the anterior region which may have less support leading it to be weaker structurally when subjected to trauma.

raw_response:
The zygomatic arch is composed of both bone and sutures. Fractures can occur along these bony or fibrous structures, but due to its prominence and structural complexity, fractures commonly involve actual bone rather than just sutural lines. The posterior portion tends to be more robustly supported by surrounding muscles and ligaments compared to the anterior region which may have less support leading it to be weaker structurally when subjected to trauma.

My Answer: C Based on anatomical structure and biomechanics, I would select option C (Posterior to zygomaticotemporal suture). This area likely experiences greater force transfer from masticatory muscles during impact and has fewer stabilizing structures compared to the anterior part, making it prone to breaking under traumatic forces. **Answer:** C Posterior to zygomaticotemporal suture.
